summary: Dan Shapiro reviews eCabinet Systems' free cabinet design software after spending a week learning it. He finds it genuinely free with no strings attached and easy to install, but plagued by bugs, crashes, and poor error handling. The UI is a mix of Windows-standard and AutoCAD-compatible conventions with questionable design choices, though he notes it's learnable. Despite the rough edges, he's enthusiastic about using it to design bookcases, and mentions his background as a lead program manager for the Windows XP UI at Microsoft.
key_points: eCabinet Systems software is genuinely free with no hidden costs, though purchasing materials through it isn't the cheapest option
The software suffers from crashes, bugs, and poor error handling — their registration server was offline for three days with no user-facing error message
The UI makes questionable choices like using buttons instead of tabs and inconsistent naming, but remains learnable
The 350+ page learning guide is invaluable and estimated to take about 10 hours to work through
Shapiro deliberately downplays his UI expertise, revealing only at the end that he was lead program manager for the Windows XP interface at Microsoft
money_quotes: It really is free; there appear to be no strings attached whatsoever.
It crashes periodically (bad), but doesn't appear to corrupt the files when it goes down (good).
I couldn't register the software for three days because their server was offline, but it just hung instead of telling me that.
I suspect Thermwood needs to hire some good dedicated testers.
All that being said, it's not completely crazy and is quite learnable.
key_insight: Even free, feature-rich software loses trust through poor error handling and UI inconsistencies — problems that dedicated testing and design attention could easily fix.
